questions.question: What is an embedded system?
questions.answer: An embedded system is a dedicated computer system designed to perform specific functions, often with real-time constraints, embedded into larger devices (e.g., microwaves, cars, medical devices).

questions.question: What is the difference between a microcontroller and a microprocessor?
questions.answer: Microcontroller includes CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O on single chip (for dedicated tasks). Microprocessor is only CPU requiring external peripherals (for general-purpose computing).

questions.question: What is an RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)?
questions.answer: An OS guaranteeing deterministic response times, managing tasks with priority-based scheduling (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, Zephyr).

questions.question: What is the difference between hard real-time and soft real-time systems?
questions.answer: Hard real-time: missing deadline causes catastrophic failure (airbag, pacemaker). Soft real-time: missed deadline degrades performance (streaming video).

questions.question: What is an interrupt?
questions.answer: A signal suspending CPU's current task to execute an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) for urgent events (I/O, timer).

questions.question: What is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)?
questions.answer: A special function executing in response to an interrupt, must be fast, short, and avoid blocking.

questions.question: What is the difference between an interrupt and a poll?
questions.answer: Interrupt: hardware notifies CPU event (efficient). Polling: CPU repeatedly checks status (wastes CPU cycles).

questions.question: What is interrupt latency?
questions.answer: Time from interrupt assertion until CPU starts executing ISR, influenced by critical sections, long instructions, or disabled interrupts.

questions.question: What is a nested interrupt?
questions.answer: Higher-priority interrupt interrupting currently executing lower-priority ISR, requiring careful stack and reentrancy handling.

questions.question: What is a watchdog timer?
questions.answer: Hardware timer resetting system if software fails to periodically 'kick' it, recovering from hangs or infinite loops.

questions.question: What is a memory-mapped I/O?
questions.answer: Peripheral registers mapped to CPU's address space, accessed using regular load/store instructions (vs port-mapped I/O).

questions.question: What is DMA (Direct Memory Access)?
questions.answer: Peripheral transferring data between memory and I/O without CPU intervention, freeing CPU for other tasks.

questions.question: What is a bootloader?
questions.answer: Code executed on startup initializing hardware, loading application firmware from storage (flash, SD card, UART) into execution memory.

questions.question: What is the difference between SRAM and DRAM in embedded systems?
questions.answer: SRAM faster, no refresh, higher cost (cache). DRAM slower, requires refresh, higher density (main memory but rare in MCUs).

questions.question: What is flash memory in microcontrollers?
questions.answer: Non-volatile memory storing firmware and persistent data, block-erasable with limited write cycles (10k-100k).

questions.question: What is EEPROM?
questions.answer: Electrically Erasable Programmable ROM, byte-addressable non-volatile memory for small configuration data (higher endurance than flash).

questions.question: What is a stack and heap in embedded C?
questions.answer: Stack: static memory for local variables, return addresses (LIFO). Heap: dynamic allocation (malloc/free), risky in embedded (fragmentation).

questions.question: What is a linker script?
questions.answer: Script defining memory layout: code (text), initialized data (data), uninitialized (bss), stack, heap placement in memory map.

questions.question: What is a volatile keyword in embedded C?
questions.answer: Prevents compiler optimization on variable, forcing re-read from memory (hardware registers, shared ISR variables).

questions.question: What is an ISR-safe function?
questions.answer: Reentrant or non-blocking function avoiding malloc, long loops, and using atomic operations to prevent corruption from nested interrupts.

questions.question: What is a race condition in embedded systems?
questions.answer: Unpredictable outcome when multiple tasks/ISRs access shared data without synchronization (e.g., mutex, atomic ops).

questions.question: What is a semaphore in RTOS?
questions.answer: Synchronization primitive controlling access to shared resources with wait (take) and signal (give) operations (binary/counting).

questions.question: What is a mutex compared to semaphore?
questions.answer: Mutex provides mutual exclusion with priority inheritance, ownership (released by same task). Semaphore may allow signaling between tasks.

questions.question: What is priority inversion?
questions.answer: Low-priority task holding resource needed by high-priority task, blocked by medium-priority task. Solved by priority inheritance protocol.

questions.question: What is a message queue in RTOS?
questions.answer: IPC mechanism passing data (messages) between tasks/ISRs, typically FIFO with blocking/timeout options.

questions.question: What is a task (thread) in RTOS?
questions.answer: Independent execution unit with its own stack, priority, and state (ready, running, blocked, suspended).

questions.question: What is task scheduling in RTOS?
questions.answer: Determining which task runs: preemptive (higher priority preempts lower), cooperative (tasks voluntarily yield), round-robin.

questions.question: What is a tick in RTOS?
questions.answer: Periodic timer interrupt (e.g., 1ms) driving scheduler, time delays, and timeouts.

questions.question: What is a context switch?
questions.answer: Saving current task's CPU state (registers, PC, SP) and restoring another task's state to resume execution.

questions.question: What is a HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)?
questions.answer: Software layer providing uniform APIs across MCU families, hiding low-level register details (e.g., CMSIS, STM32 HAL).

questions.question: What is a GPIO (General Purpose Input Output) pin?
questions.answer: Configurable pin for digital input (read button), output (LED), alternate function (UART, SPI, PWM), or interrupt.

questions.question: What is PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)?
questions.answer: Technique varying duty cycle of square wave for analog-like control: motor speed, LED dimming, servo position.

questions.question: What is an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)?
questions.answer: Converts analog voltage to digital value (e.g., 12-bit = 0-4095), used for sensors (temperature, potentiometer).

questions.question: What is a DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)?
questions.answer: Converts digital value to analog voltage (e.g., for audio output, waveform generation).

questions.question: What is UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)?
questions.answer: Serial communication protocol with two wires (TX, RX) for point-to-point, configurable baud rate, parity, stop bits.

questions.question: What is I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)?
questions.answer: Two-wire bus (SDA, SCL) for multi-slave communication with addresses, supporting 100kbps-3.4Mbps.

questions.question: What is SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)?
questions.answer: Full-duplex synchronous bus with 4 wires (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CS), master-slave, higher speed than I2C.

questions.question: What is the difference between I2C and SPI?
questions.answer: I2C uses 2 wires with addressing, ACK, multi-master (slower). SPI uses 4 wires, no addressing, CS per slave (faster, simpler).

questions.question: What is CAN (Controller Area Network)?
questions.answer: Robust differential bus protocol for automotive/industrial with collision detection, error handling, and priority arbitration.

questions.question: What is RS-232 vs RS-485?
questions.answer: RS-232: unbalanced, single-ended, short distance (15m). RS-485: differential, long distance (1200m), multi-drop.

questions.question: What is a CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)?
questions.answer: Error-detecting code calculating checksum for data integrity, used in communication and storage.

questions.question: What is a FIFO (First In First Out) buffer?
questions.answer: Queue structure for buffering data between producer and consumer (UART RX/TX, DMA), preventing data loss.

questions.question: What is a circular buffer?
questions.answer: Fixed-size buffer wrapping around using head/tail pointers, efficient for streaming data (logging, audio).

questions.question: What is bit-banging?
questions.answer: Software emulation of communication protocol (I2C, SPI) by directly toggling GPIO pins, bypassing hardware peripherals.

questions.question: What is endianness in embedded systems?
questions.answer: Byte order: little-endian (LSB first, ARM default), big-endian (MSB first) affecting data interpretation across systems.

questions.question: What is memory alignment?
questions.answer: Data addresses matching their size (e.g., 4-byte aligned for 32-bit word), avoiding performance penalties or faults.

questions.question: What is a bit-field in embedded C?
questions.answer: Structure packing bits for register flags reducing memory, but compiler-dependent ordering and alignment.

questions.question: What is a register bit masking?
questions.answer: Manipulating specific bits using AND/OR/XOR with masks (e.g., `REG |= (1<<3)` sets bit 3 without affecting others).

questions.question: What is an atomic operation?
questions.answer: Operation completing without interruption, crucial for shared variables (use `__atomic_` builtins or disable interrupts).

questions.question: What is memory-mapped file vs memory-mapped I/O?
questions.answer: Memory-mapped file maps file to memory (storage). Memory-mapped I/O maps peripherals to address space (hardware registers).

questions.question: What is a JTAG (Joint Test Action Group)?
questions.answer: Debug interface for programming, breakpoints, memory inspection, and boundary scan testing (4-5 pins: TDI, TDO, TCK, TMS, optional TRST).

questions.question: What is SWD (Serial Wire Debug)?
questions.answer: Two-pin debug interface (SWDIO, SWCLK) replacing JTAG for ARM Cortex-M, smaller pin footprint.

questions.question: What is in-circuit emulator (ICE) vs debugger?
questions.answer: ICE replaces CPU for full control (expensive). Debugger uses on-chip debug logic (cheaper, common).

questions.question: What is a breakpoint?
questions.answer: Debugging mechanism pausing program execution at specified address; hardware (limited) or software (instruction replacement).

questions.question: What is a watchpoint?
questions.answer: Breakpoint triggered when memory address is read/written, useful for tracking variable changes.

questions.question: What is an instrumentation trace macrocell (ITM)?
questions.answer: ARM debug feature outputting printf-style debug messages over SWO pin without halting CPU.

questions.question: What is a logic analyzer?
questions.answer: Hardware tool capturing multiple digital signals over time, useful for debugging protocol timing and glitches.

questions.question: What is an oscilloscope in embedded debugging?
questions.answer: Analog waveform measurement for voltage levels, rise/fall times, noise, and power integrity.

questions.question: What is a power budget in embedded design?
questions.answer: Total power consumption across components (MCU, sensors, wireless) dictating battery life and thermal management.

questions.question: What are sleep modes in microcontrollers?
questions.answer: Low-power states: idle (CPU off, peripherals on), stop (most clocks off), standby (lowest leakage, wake from few sources).

questions.question: What is a real-time clock (RTC)?
questions.answer: Low-power timer tracking wall-clock time (seconds, minutes, date) with battery backup, generating periodic wakeup interrupts.

questions.question: What is brown-out detection (BOD)?
questions.answer: Circuit resetting MCU when supply voltage drops below threshold, preventing memory corruption or undefined behavior.

questions.question: What is a phase-locked loop (PLL)?
questions.answer: Frequency multiplier generating high-speed system clock from low-frequency external crystal (e.g., 8MHz → 168MHz).

questions.question: What is clock gating in embedded systems?
questions.answer: Disabling clock to idle peripherals reducing dynamic power consumption, common in low-power MCUs.

questions.question: What is a watchdog timer in safety systems?
questions.answer: Independent timer ensuring software health; failure to reset triggers safe state (reset, alarm, switch-off).

questions.question: What is IEC 61508 standard?
questions.answer: Functional safety standard for electrical/electronic systems, defining Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) for embedded systems.

questions.question: What is MISRA C?
questions.answer: Guidelines for safe, reliable C code in embedded systems (e.g., rule 12.4: no side effects in logical &&, ||).

questions.question: What is a static code analyzer?
questions.answer: Tool checking code against MISRA, CERT standards, detecting bugs (null dereference, overflow) without execution.

questions.question: What is a failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA)?
questions.answer: Systematic method analyzing potential failure modes, effects, and criticality for risk reduction.

questions.question: What is a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test?
questions.answer: Testing embedded software with real hardware stimulated by simulated environment, validating real-time behavior.

questions.question: What is a firmware over-the-air (FOTA) update?
questions.answer: Wirelessly updating device firmware (IoT) using delta/differential updates, rollback, and integrity checks.

questions.question: What is an A/B partition update strategy?
questions.answer: Two flash partitions: run from A while writing update to B, then swap on next boot; allows recovery if update fails.

questions.question: What is a checksum vs hash in firmware validation?
questions.answer: Checksum (CRC) detects accidental errors. Cryptographic hash (SHA-256) ensures integrity and authenticity (secure boot).

questions.question: What is secure boot?
questions.answer: Bootloader verifying firmware signature using public key before execution, preventing unauthorized code.

questions.question: What is a trustzone for Arm Cortex-M?
questions.answer: Hardware security isolation splitting MCU into secure (key storage, crypto) and non-secure (application) worlds.

questions.question: What is address space layout randomization (ASLR) in embedded?
questions.answer: Randomizing memory locations to prevent code reuse attacks (limited in MCUs without MMU).

questions.question: What is an MPU (Memory Protection Unit)?
questions.answer: Hardware enforcing memory regions with access permissions (read/write/execute) and privileged/user modes.

questions.question: What is an MMU (Memory Management Unit)?
questions.answer: Virtual memory mapping used in embedded Linux (Cortex-A), absent in most MCUs (Cortex-M).

questions.question: What is a real-time clock drift?
questions.answer: Crystal frequency deviation causing RTC error over time (e.g., ±20ppm = ±1.7 seconds/day).

questions.question: What is jitter in embedded systems?
questions.answer: Variation in timing between expected and actual event occurrence (interrupt latency jitter, task release jitter).

questions.question: What is deterministic latency?
questions.answer: Predictable, bounded worst-case execution time (WCET) essential for hard real-time systems.

questions.question: What is a worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis?
questions.answer: Computing maximum time function/task takes using static analysis or measurement, ensuring deadlines met.

questions.question: What is a rate monotonic scheduling (RMS) algorithm?
questions.answer: Priority assignment: shorter period tasks get higher priority, optimal for periodic tasks with deadlines equal periods.

questions.question: What is earliest deadline first (EDF) scheduling?
questions.answer: Dynamic priority scheduling executing task with earliest absolute deadline, higher utilization than RMS.

questions.question: What is a deadlock in embedded systems?
questions.answer: Two tasks each holding resource needed by other, causing indefinite wait. Prevented by mutex ordering or timeout.

questions.question: What is a livelock?
questions.answer: Tasks continuously changing state but making no progress (e.g., retrying conflicting operation forever).

questions.question: What is a circular buffer overflow?
questions.answer: Writing beyond buffer capacity overwriting oldest data (intentional ring buffer) or newer data (bug) causing corruption.

questions.question: What is a stack overflow in embedded systems?
questions.answer: Stack exceeding allocated memory corrupting heap/bss. Detected by watermark (canary), MPU, or stack pointers.

questions.question: What is a heap fragmentation?
questions.answer: Small free blocks scattered making large allocation fail even if total free memory sufficient (avoid dynamic allocation in critical systems).

questions.question: What is a power-on reset (POR) circuit?
questions.answer: Generates reset pulse when supply voltage rises above threshold, ensuring MCU starts in known state.

questions.question: What is a system tick timer?
questions.answer: Simple periodic timer (SysTick in ARM) generating RTOS tick or delay loops.

questions.question: What is a high-resolution timer (HRTIM)?
questions.answer: Advanced timer for PWM, capture/compare, dead-time insertion, used in motor control and power conversion.

questions.question: What is motor control PWM (six-step vs FOC)?
questions.answer: Six-step (trapezoidal) simpler, hall sensors. Field-Oriented Control (FOC) sinusoidal, efficient, torque control.

questions.question: What is a quadrature encoder?
questions.answer: Sensor outputting two square waves (A, B) with phase shift indicating direction and position of rotating shaft.

questions.question: What is a Hall effect sensor?
questions.answer: Magnetic field sensor detecting rotor position in BLDC motors or simple proximity sensing.

questions.question: What is a capacitive touch sensing?
questions.answer: Measuring capacitance change when finger touches pad (self-capacitance vs mutual-capacitance matrix).

questions.question: What is an accelerometer vs gyroscope?
questions.answer: Accelerometer measures linear acceleration (tilt, shock). Gyroscope measures angular velocity (rotation rate).

questions.question: What is a MEMS sensor?
questions.answer: Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (small mechanical structures etched on silicon) for inertial, pressure, microphones.

questions.question: What is a NAND Flash vs NOR Flash?
questions.answer: NAND higher density, block access, cheaper (mass storage). NOR random access, execute-in-place (XIP, firmware storage).

questions.question: What is a SBC (Single Board Computer) vs microcontroller board?
questions.answer: SBC runs OS (Linux, Android) with MPU, RAM, storage (Raspberry Pi). MCU board runs bare metal/RTOS (Arduino, STM32 Nucleo).
